NZ Office of the Ombudsman DoView Board

Proof of Concept - Not Endorsed. This AI-assisted draft maps how the Office’s services and capabilities may contribute to its current strategic outcomes. It uses public information, chiefly the Strategic Intentions 2025–2030 and Annual Report 2024/2025. It is not an official organisation chart, legal interpretation or performance assessment.

pageInfo.p1: Public awareness, accessible information and engagement help people understand rights and take constructive action. Source: https://www.ombudsman.parliament.nz/resources/chief-ombudsmans-strategic-intentions-2025-2030
pageInfo.p2: Capability-building includes advice, guidance, training, proactive intervention and learning from complaint patterns. Source: https://www.ombudsman.parliament.nz/resources/chief-ombudsmans-strategic-intentions-2025-2030
pageInfo.p3: The OIA pathway combines lawful agency handling with independent review and correction. Sources: https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/1982/0156/latest/DLM65608.html and https://www.ombudsman.parliament.nz/resources/chief-ombudsmans-strategic-intentions-2025-2030
pageInfo.p4: The Ombudsman may investigate administrative conduct by complaint or own-motion initiative and seek practical or systemic resolution. Sources: https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/1975/0009/latest/DLM431166.html and https://www.ombudsman.parliament.nz/resources/chief-ombudsmans-strategic-intentions-2025-2030
pageInfo.p5: This page models independent, child-centred oversight of Oranga Tamariki and care or custody providers at a high level. Source: https://www.ombudsman.parliament.nz/what-ombudsman-can-help
pageInfo.p6: The protected-disclosure pathway combines information, confidentiality, assessment, referral or investigation, and protection from retaliation. Sources: https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/2022/0020/latest/LMS310194.html and https://www.ombudsman.parliament.nz/resources/overview-protected-disclosures-protection-whistleblowers-act-2022
pageInfo.p7: The Ombudsman monitors Disability Convention implementation with Independent Monitoring Mechanism partners and investigates relevant public-sector complaints. Source: https://www.ombudsman.parliament.nz/what-ombudsman-can-help/fair-treatment-disabled-people
pageInfo.p8: Preventive monitoring covers prisons, court facilities, mental-health, intellectual-disability, aged-care and immigration-detention settings. Sources: https://www.ombudsman.parliament.nz/what-ombudsman-can-help/monitoring-places-detention and https://www.legislation.govt.nz/act/public/1989/0106/latest/dlm192818.html
pageInfo.p9: International collaboration is included because the current strategic framework treats reciprocal learning and integrity-institution development as a distinct output. Source: https://www.ombudsman.parliament.nz/resources/chief-ombudsmans-strategic-intentions-2025-2030
pageInfo.p10: Level 1 maps public-facing and statutory services/workstreams to the results they directly and materially influence.
pageInfo.p11: Level 2 uses functional capability labels for alignment analysis; these are not asserted to be formal team names or reporting lines.
pageInfo.p12: No-level cross-cutting competencies map to relevant This–Then and numbered How boxes through Cross-Links.
pageInfo.final: Final Outcomes reflect the current strategic goal and high-level outcomes. Source: https://www.ombudsman.parliament.nz/resources/chief-ombudsmans-strategic-intentions-2025-2030

docContent.p13:
Purpose

This proof-of-concept board is an AI-assisted draft of the Office of the Ombudsman’s outcome pathways, services, enabling functions and cross-cutting competencies. It is grounded primarily in the Strategic Intentions 2025–2030 and the Annual Report 2024/2025.

Scope

The board covers public information, public-sector capability, official information, administrative fairness, children in care, protected disclosures, disability rights, detention oversight and international integrity work. It separates causal results on This–Then Pages from services and capabilities on How Pages.

Assumptions

  • Level 2 labels describe functional capabilities for alignment analysis; they do not claim an official current team structure.
  • Box-to-box relationships are draft causal propositions, not findings of effectiveness or legal conclusions.
  • Te ao Māori, accessibility, independence, proportionality, confidentiality and human-rights practice are treated as cross-cutting requirements.
  • The board should be reviewed with Office staff, affected communities, public agencies, Parliament-facing stakeholders and subject-matter experts.
docContent.p14:
Primary strategic sources

  • Strategic Intentions 2025–2030: current goal, outcomes, impacts, outputs and enabling capabilities.
  • Annual Report 2024/2025: recent demand, delivery, timeliness and proactive-oversight context.
  • What the Ombudsman can help with: public-facing jurisdiction and service routes.

Statutory and specialist sources

  • Ombudsmen Act 1975.
  • Official Information Act 1982.
  • Protected Disclosures (Protection of Whistleblowers) Act 2022 and Ombudsman guidance.
  • Crimes of Torture Act 1989 and the Office’s detention-monitoring overview.
  • The disability-rights overview explains the Ombudsman’s role in the Independent Monitoring Mechanism and use of lived-experience advice.

Evidence status

Sources establish statutory roles, stated strategy and reported activity. They do not by themselves prove every causal link in this draft. Relationship-level evidence should be strengthened during expert review and evaluation.
docContent.p15:
Interpretation cautions

  • This is a draft planning and alignment model, not an endorsed Office strategy, legal opinion, performance assessment or representation of delegations.
  • Formal powers and jurisdiction vary by statute. Compact boxes do not replace the governing legislation or current Ombudsman guidance.
  • Higher complaint volumes may reflect awareness, accessibility, external conditions or unresolved agency issues; they are not a simple performance signal.
  • Timeliness, quality, proportionality, confidentiality, fairness and impact need to be considered together.
  • Recommendations may contribute to change alongside agency leadership, policy, law, funding, workforce and wider system conditions.
  • Population-level trust and integrity outcomes are influenced by many institutions beyond the Ombudsman.
  • Public source material was current when researched on 20 July 2026. Recheck legislation, strategy, organisation and performance targets before operational use.

Human review

Review should include legal and jurisdictional checks, operational validation, Māori and disability perspectives, lived experience, privacy and security review, and confirmation of measurement feasibility.
docContent.p16:
Illustrative monitoring and evaluation plan

Use a small balanced set of timeliness, quality, accessibility, implementation and contribution measures. Treat measures as prompts for learning rather than a substitute for judgement.

Example pathway

Core measures

Learning questions

Review rhythm

  • Quarterly: review demand, timeliness, accessibility and quality together.
  • Six-monthly: examine recommendation implementation, repeated failures and emerging systemic risks.
  • Annually: test contribution to fair treatment, informed participation, public trust and robust independent oversight.
docContent.p17:
Mapping method

Every Level 1 source was compared with all 117 This–Then boxes, and every This–Then target was reverse-checked against all 12 Level 1 sources. Every Level 2 capability was compared with all Level 1 services. Each non-level competency was reviewed against This–Then and numbered How boxes. Only upward, direct and material relationships were serialized.

Source-side and target-side review

The source-side review asks what each service or capability directly delivers, enables, governs, assures or strengthens. The target-side review asks which services or capabilities materially contribute to each result. One incoming link was never treated as proof of complete mapping.

Concentrations and gaps

  • Data-led proactive monitoring, agency capability support, and follow-up/impact assessment have broad cross-page influence; their higher degree is expected but requires expert confirmation.
  • Specialist statutory services concentrate on their own pathways, while reporting, accessibility and systems-learning work cut across several pathways.
  • Results with no mapped direct Level 1 support at build time: none.
  • Level 1 workstreams with one or no mapped Level 2 contributors: none.
  • Possible overlap requiring review: administrative complaints, children-in-care oversight, disability complaints and proactive systemic monitoring; clarify hand-offs without collapsing distinct statutory roles.

Uncertainty requiring review

Public sources do not provide a definitive current internal team-to-service matrix. Level 2 therefore models functional capabilities, not reporting lines. Cross-cutting competency links indicate where expertise appears materially necessary; they do not assert staffing levels or ownership.

How to use counts

Link counts are indicators for alignment analysis only. They do not prove staffing, expenditure, accountability, ownership, effort, causal strength or organisational performance. A low count may reflect a specialised role; a high count may identify a genuinely broad capability or an over-broad mapping that should be challenged.

Required human review

The mapping requires review by appropriate domain experts, Office representatives, affected stakeholders, Māori, disabled and Deaf people, people with experience of detention or care, public-sector agencies and Parliament-facing stakeholders.
docContent.p18:
Using DoView Boards and Disclaimer

See the disclaimer section below.

The DoView Board prompt and the DoView Board built within this DoView Board prototype app are provided free of charge so that anyone can experiment with DoView Boards and explore for themselves how they can answer the 20 key questions that anyone running or overseeing an organization needs to answer. See https://doviewplanning.org/doviewboardsuse.

Walk-Through

To get a walk-through for using DoView Boards, see https://doviewplanning.org/walkthrough.

Accessing Consulting or Training

We can provide consulting or training on integrating the use of DoView Boards into your existing planning, implementation and reporting workflows in business, government, or nonprofit settings. See https://doviewplanning.org/offerings.

Adapting the DoView Board Prototype App to your setting

This DoView Board is being used within the DoView Board prototype app. It is a free of charge app that runs inside any browser window and can be used for piloting, testing, proof-of-concept and for use where confidential information is not being included and where there is a low security risk.

The DoView Board app is an open-source developer project under an Apache-2.0 open-source license. See https://github.com/doviewplanning/official-doview-boards. The wider DoView Planning methodology and DoView® trademarks are governed separately. See https://doviewplanning.org/trademarkuse.

Because it is an open-source project, any developer can extend, harden, adapt, or include features of the DoView Board app in other planning products or systems to suit specific deployment requirements. For example, adding enhanced stability, scalability, security, authentication, data handling, or integration features needed for production use, sensitive content, or higher-security environments. If you are looking to deploy DoView Boards in a live operational setting, you can work with any developer to tailor the app to your needs. You can also get in touch with us for advice and methodological input into the best way for you to adapt and extend your use of DoView Boards in your particular setting. https://doviewplanning.org/contact.

Disclaimer

DoView Boards are provided for planning and illustrative purposes only. The content of any DoView Board does not constitute professional advice of any kind, including but not limited to legal, financial, medical, strategic, or organizational advice. No warranty is given as to the accuracy, completeness, or fitness for purpose of any content. The usual precautions for AI-generated material need to be taken. Dr Paul Duignan, DoViewPlanning.org, The Ideas Web Ltd, DoView Corporation Ltd and any associated parties accept no liability whatsoever for any loss, damage, or adverse outcome arising directly or indirectly from the use of, or reliance on, any DoView Board or its contents. Use entirely at your own risk.

Please note that this DoView Board is in an interactive HTML file and contains active JavaScript so the board can work. Only open boards from sources you trust. Read-only copies disable editing through the board interface but are not security protection.

measures.title: Public awareness of the Ombudsman and key rights
measures.mainText: Track overall awareness and differences across communities.
measures.notes1: Use representative research and accessible engagement feedback.

measures.title: Engagement participants reporting improved understanding
measures.mainText: Assess whether outreach leaves people better able to act.

measures.title: Complaints completed within relevant timeliness standards
measures.mainText: Report by jurisdiction and case pathway, alongside quality safeguards.

measures.title: Complainants reporting fair and clear handling
measures.mainText: Measure fairness, communication and usefulness, not only speed.

measures.title: Recommendations and suggestions accepted or implemented
measures.mainText: Track implementation, time to completion and sustained practice change.

measures.title: Official-information matters resolved and corrected
measures.mainText: Separate early resolution, investigation and agency practice change.

measures.title: Protected-disclosure matters completed to quality standards
measures.mainText: Pair completion and timeliness with confidentiality and protection checks.

measures.title: Accessible resources produced and tested with users
measures.mainText: Include NZSL and alternative formats and assess practical usability.

measures.title: Detention inspections and visits completed
measures.mainText: Disaggregate by setting, visit type and whether announced.

measures.title: Detention findings issued within planned timeframes
measures.mainText: Track prompt feedback without trading off evidential quality.

measures.title: Repeat administrative failures reduced
measures.mainText: Use complaint themes and follow-up evidence to identify recurrence.

measures.title: Systemic improvements attributable to Ombudsman intervention
measures.mainText: Use contribution evidence; do not claim sole attribution.

evalQuestions.questionText: Which communities remain less aware of the Ombudsman or unable to use its services?
evalQuestions.mainText: Examine awareness, accessibility, trust and channel barriers.

evalQuestions.questionText: Which information and engagement approaches most often lead to constructive action?
evalQuestions.mainText: Compare formats and pathways while accounting for community context.

evalQuestions.questionText: How and when does agency guidance change frontline administrative practice?
evalQuestions.mainText: Trace uptake from advice through procedures to decisions affecting people.

evalQuestions.questionText: Which official-information interventions improve timeliness without weakening lawful decision-making?
evalQuestions.mainText: Compare early resolution, formal investigation and proactive monitoring.

evalQuestions.questionText: Do complaint resolutions provide practical remedies that affected people value?
evalQuestions.mainText: Test usefulness, fairness and durability of the resolution.

evalQuestions.questionText: Can tamariki and rangatahi in care raise concerns safely and obtain an effective response?
evalQuestions.mainText: Centre direct experience and examine provider follow-through.

evalQuestions.questionText: Are confidentiality, non-retaliation and support arrangements working for disclosers?
evalQuestions.mainText: Look beyond formal procedure to discloser experience and consequences.

evalQuestions.questionText: Which intersecting barriers most restrict disabled and Deaf people’s participation?
evalQuestions.mainText: Use lived experience, complaints and monitoring evidence together.

evalQuestions.questionText: Are detention recommendations implemented and sustained across different settings?
evalQuestions.mainText: Examine follow-up evidence and recurring risks, not acceptance alone.

evalQuestions.questionText: What international learning has materially improved Ombudsman practice in New Zealand?
evalQuestions.mainText: Trace adapted practice and reciprocal benefit rather than activity volume.

evalQuestions.questionText: How well are te ao Māori and Te Tiriti considerations reflected in oversight practice?
evalQuestions.mainText: Seek review from Māori stakeholders and examine practical influence.

evalQuestions.questionText: How do timeliness improvements affect quality, fairness and complainant experience?
evalQuestions.mainText: Test for trade-offs across case pathways and communities.

boxes: Tamariki and rangatahi know where to complain
People in power act with integrity
Disabled and Deaf people’s experiences heard

FO: People informed and views genuinely considered
Parliament assured of robust independent oversight
